Q1: Honestly, my study routine was kind of all over the place. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings to listen to lecture recordings since I learn best by hearing things, but with my extracurriculars and everything else going on, I often couldn't stick to a consistent schedule. I'd usually end up doing intense study sessions when I could fit them in, maybe around 13 hours a week total, but spread out unpredictably. I always had good intentions to be more organized, but life kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else I had going on. Some weeks I'd barely touch my coursework because I was busy with activities or just feeling too anxious to start. Then when deadlines approached, I'd cram everything in at once. I wish I could say I had a regular schedule, but the reality is that my studying was pretty reactive rather than proactive.
Q3: Usually what got me to log into the LMS was either a notification about something due soon or remembering that I needed to check on an assignment. Sometimes the gamified tasks would pull me in because they felt less intimidating than the regular coursework. If I'm being honest, a lot of times I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which sometimes made my anxiety worse rather than better.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in became more routine, especially when I was keeping up with the gamified activities. Those felt more manageable and gave me small wins that kept me coming back. But that routine never really stuck for the whole semester. My anxiety would spike, I'd fall behind, and then logging in felt more like facing my failures than actually learning.
Q5: The recorded lectures were honestly my lifeline because I could listen to them while doing other things or replay parts I didn't understand. I'm an auditory learner, so being able to hear explanations multiple times was huge for me. The gamified tasks were also really helpful because they broke things down into smaller, less overwhelming pieces. They made me feel like I was actually accomplishing something even when the bigger assignments felt impossible.
Q6: I definitely ignored the discussion posts most of the time. I only posted four times all semester because putting my thoughts out there for everyone to see made me really anxious. The readings I'd often skip or just skim because reading isn't how I learn best anyway. I knew I should engage more with those resources, but I kept telling myself I'd get to them later, and later never came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate progress or when it connected clearly to what would be on the exam. The gamified tasks were great for this because I got instant feedback and could see myself moving forward. If something felt too abstract or I couldn't tell how it would help me, I'd put it off. I needed to feel like my limited energy was being spent on something that would actually make a difference.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. When I was already struggling to complete the required work, doing extra stuff seemed impossible. But weirdly, sometimes the optional activities were less stressful because there was no pressure to perform perfectly. The required ones carried all this weight of affecting my grade, which made my anxiety spike every time I approached them.
Q9: Oh absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download the audio from lectures and listen to them on my phone while commuting or exercising. I also had a study group with some classmates where we'd talk through concepts together in person. Plus I used some YouTube videos and podcasts to supplement what we were learning. A lot of my actual learning happened outside that platform.
Q10: There were many times when I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't reflect it at all. Like when I'd listen to downloaded lectures repeatedly while doing chores, or when my study group would meet at the library and we'd work through problems together on a whiteboard. I also spent time just thinking through concepts while walking between classes. None of that shows up anywhere, but it was real learning time.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy for video content. I'd watch or download something once from the LMS, then keep it on my devices to review later without logging back in. It was more convenient and I could access it without internet sometimes. I probably watched certain lecture segments five or six times, but the system only recorded that initial access.
Q12: There were definitely periods where engaging became really hard, especially around midterms when everything piled up at once. My anxiety would get so bad that I'd freeze up and couldn't bring myself to even open the LMS. I'd know I was falling behind, which made the anxiety worse, which made it even harder to engage. It was this terrible cycle that I couldn't seem to break out of.
Q13: All of those things affected me, honestly. The workload felt overwhelming sometimes, and when I got confused about something, I'd spiral into anxiety rather than asking for help. Fatigue was a big factor too because my extracurriculars took a lot of energy. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in and just stare at the screen without really processing anything, or I'd give up and close the tab entirely.
Q14: This happened more often than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through course materials without actually reading them, just going through the motions. Sometimes I was so anxious that even though I was technically accessing the content, nothing was sinking in. It was like being physically present but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even though I knew they were important. My assignment completion rate was really low because of this. Part of it was perfectionism and anxiety - I'd rather not submit something than submit something imperfect. Part of it was just feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. The longer I waited, the scarier the task became, which made me avoid it even more.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. I'd have these bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming everything in at the last minute. The problem was that I'd spend so much energy on the deadline panic that I'd be exhausted afterward and fall behind on the next thing. It wasn't a healthy pattern, but it was the only way I could push through my anxiety sometimes.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more. My assignment scores were actually really high when I did submit work, which felt validating. But sometimes feedback came too late to feel relevant, or it was critical in a way that just increased my anxiety. I needed encouragement to keep going, and when I got it, I did better. When I didn't, I'd retreat.
Q18: The course structure was honestly a mixed bag for me. Having eight online courses meant a lot of flexibility, which I needed for my schedule, but also meant less accountability. The gamified elements really helped because they gave me structure and small goals. But the overall pacing sometimes felt relentless - like I could never fully catch up once I fell behind, which was discouraging.
Q19: The gamified tasks definitely made engagement easier because they felt achievable and even fun sometimes. What made it harder was when assignments were vague about expectations or when there was too much reading-based content instead of audio options. Also, the discussion requirements stressed me out because I'm not comfortable putting my thoughts out publicly. More flexibility in how we could demonstrate learning would have helped.
Q20: I can totally relate to that pattern. Sometimes you're logging in constantly because you're anxious and checking on things, or you're exploring and trying to understand what's expected, but you can't bring yourself to actually submit anything. The submission part requires a different kind of courage than just browsing. For me, high activity often meant high anxiety, not high productivity.
Q21: That makes sense to me too, actually. Some people are just efficient - they know what they need, they get it, and they're done. Or they're doing a lot of learning outside the system that doesn't get tracked. I think my LMS activity was moderate, but my actual learning was happening in different ways. If I'd been better at submitting work, my grades would have reflected what I actually knew better.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active or how often you access things, which might indicate stress or deadline-driven behavior. But they completely miss the quality of engagement - whether you're actually learning or just clicking around anxiously. They also miss all the offline studying, group work, and the emotional experience of being a student. Numbers don't capture struggle or growth.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and thinking about the material, not just going through required motions. It's when I find myself connecting ideas to other things in my life or wanting to learn more beyond what's assigned. True engagement feels like curiosity and investment, not obligation. Unfortunately, my anxiety often got in the way of feeling that genuine engagement, even when I wanted to care.
Q24: The LMS data wouldn't show how much I actually learned through listening and discussing with peers offline. It wouldn't show the nights I stayed up worrying about the course or the genuine effort I put into assignments when I did complete them. It also wouldn't capture that despite failing, I actually understood a lot of the material - I just couldn't demonstrate it through the required channels because of my anxiety and avoidance patterns.
